title: Navigation Redesign
pitch: Analytics showed customers were overwhelmingly clicking primary categories and largely ignoring the subcategory depth the mega menus maintained. The redesign leaned into that behavior and added a wayfinding layer on top of it. On desktop, the mega menu collapsed into a simpler layout, with the active primary category underlined in the header and the customer's current scope underlined inside the menu itself. On mobile, every primary category was surfaced as a persistent row at the top so customers could jump between them without restarting, state was preserved between opens, the menu auto-expanded to the last primary a customer was browsing, and the secondary category was underlined to orient anyone dropped mid-site from CRM or paid media.
problem: The navigation had been built up over time to accommodate an expanding catalog, and it punished anyone who didn't arrive at the homepage. On mobile especially, the drill-down reset to square one every time the menu opened, forcing customers to re-traverse the hierarchy on every visit. The accordion pattern on the secondary page buried useful links under subcategory noise, and there was no visual cue telling a customer dropped in from paid or CRM where they actually were in the site. Non-linear traffic had no way to orient itself.
hypothesis: If the navigation matched real click behavior and showed customers where they were, wayfinding would improve for linear and non-linear traffic alike: fewer resets on mobile, fewer lost customers arriving mid-funnel, and less time spent re-orienting instead of shopping.
